title: new operator
body:
## Definition

The **`new`** operator creates an object, wires its `[[Prototype]]` from the constructor's `prototype`, binds `this`, runs the constructor, and returns the object (unless constructor returns an object).

## Why it exists

Standard pattern for instance creation before class syntax.

## How it works

```js
function Person(name) {
  this.name = name;
}
Person.prototype.greet = function () { return this.name; };

const ada = new Person("Ada");
```

Equivalent to `class` instance creation under the hood.

## Common confusion

Forgetting `new` on a constructor leaves `this` as `undefined` (strict) or the global object (sloppy). Arrow functions cannot be used as constructors.

## In practice

Modern code uses `class`; understanding `new` explains [prototype chain](concept:prototype-chain) interview questions.
bodyLinked:
## Definition

The **[new](concept:new-operator)** operator creates an object, wires its [[[Prototype]]](concept:prototype-chain) from the constructor's [prototype](concept:prototype-chain), [binds](concept:call-apply-bind) [this](concept:this-binding), runs the constructor, and returns the object (unless constructor returns an object).

## Why it exists

Standard pattern for instance creation before [class syntax](concept:es6-class).

## How it works

```js
function Person(name) {
  this.name = name;
}
Person.prototype.greet = function () { return this.name; };

const ada = new Person("Ada");
```

Equivalent to [class](concept:es6-class) instance creation under the hood.

## Common confusion

Forgetting [new](concept:new-operator) on a constructor leaves [this](concept:this-binding) as [undefined](concept:undefined) (strict) or the global object (sloppy). [Arrow functions](concept:arrow-function) cannot be used as constructors.

## In practice

Modern code uses [class](concept:es6-class); understanding [new](concept:new-operator) explains [prototype chain](concept:prototype-chain) interview questions.
